channel.title: Theory on emsenn.net
channel.description: Recent content in Theory on emsenn.net

item.title: Accepting the Web as a Platform, not a Place
item.description:
• History
    • first the internet
    • then web
    • commerce
    • platforms
  • internal mental image of web presence didn’t change from the internet days
    • personal website
    • platforms point back there
  • practiced denial of change
    • out-of-step with the theory
    • out-of-step with normal practices
      • even “The Internet” has become a platform (IndieWeb, ActivityPub)
    • out-of-step with my own practices
  • compare to living on a land in a territory, to living as a subject in an environment
    • different values, different modes of living
    • personal websites as living on a land
    • platform presence as subject in environment
    • different modes of governance
  • self-governing as required for both
    • but different governance technologies apply:
      • personal websites
        • permanence, backlinks, “sediment”, foundation, structure
      • platform
        • fungibility, presence, ???
    • practices of self-governance for platformized web life?

item.title: On Relationality
item.description:
On Relationality

A trace of the reductive tension of nothing

Introduction

The question “Why does anything exist at all?” smuggles in two opposing recognitions: Relating (to ask) and Not Relating (to deny). These recognitions cannot both vanish, so the trace below simply records the acts forced by resolving that tension. Each subsequent section earns a new act by showing how it necessarily follows from the previous one.

Style Guide

In text, Relational Terms are written in Strong Emphasized Title Case when they are being defined, and Emphasize Title Case when they are being referenced, with a parentheticized MonospacePascalCase representation of how they will be used in our relational expression format. For example, “A Relational Term (RelationalTerm) is symbol used to represent a Recognition.” After being defined in prose, the term must be given concretely in a code block.

item.title: What Is Semiotics?
item.description:
What Is Semiotics?

Studying Signs, Not Just Symbols

Semiotics is the study of signs and sense‑making.

It asks a deceptively simple question:

How do things (marks, sounds, gestures, documents, systems) come to mean something for someone, in some situation?

Instead of treating meaning as a black box, semiotics turns it into something we can inspect, model, and steward.

1. Signs, Interpreters, Contexts

Semiotics usually starts from three ingredients:

  • a sign — something that stands for something else,
  • an object — what the sign is about,
  • an interpreter — someone or something for whom the sign functions.

Classic frameworks differ in emphasis:
